exemplar.title: To what extent have recent investments for the improvement of the first 1.000 days of local childrenâs lives had positive externalities on the community of Val del Ceno, Italy?

subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Topic is communicated accurately with a clear focus on first-1000-day investments and positive externalities in Val del Ceno.
Research question is explicitly stated and effectively connected to the discussion throughout the report.
Methodology outlines both secondary and primary data with clear evidence of informed source selection.
weaknesses: Minor digression into neurodevelopment background reduces methodological tightness.
Range of methods is moderate (single survey, no triangulating data sources beyond Heckman papers and WHO).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (topic communication and research question) clearly meet the 5â6 band, while methodology sits at the upper end of the 3â4 band. Averaging these performances yields a solid 5, reflecting effective focus and method with only minor lapses.

strengths: Key secondary literature (Heckman ROI estimates, WHO framework) is integrated into diagrams and analysis.
Economic terminology (MSB, MPC, LRAS, PPC) is used accurately and consistently.
Clear understanding of market failure and positive externality mechanics is demonstrated.
weaknesses: Occasional reliance on less scholarly sources (Wikipedia, YouTube) undermines rigour.
Extended narrative on brain development is descriptive rather than tightly linked to economic analysis.
Minor notation and citation inconsistencies (e.g., typographical slips in equations).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Application of source material and disciplinary knowledge largely fit the 3â4 band, while terminology achieves a 5. On balance, a score of 4 reflects mostly relevant, coherent understanding with scope for deeper scholarly integration and tighter economic focus.

strengths: Research evidence (survey data, demographic statistics, Heckman studies) is appropriate and clearly relevant to the research question.
Structured argument links theory, context, and data in a logical progression.
Limitations (sample-size bias, methodological restrictions) are acknowledged and partially evaluated.
weaknesses: Analysis lacks numerical quantification of external benefits and cost-savings.
Evaluation is partial â alternative explanations and robustness checks are not fully explored.
Causal inferences are asserted rather than rigorously tested with empirical data.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality lies at the top of the 4â6 band, while analysis depth and evaluation are mid-band. Overall performance exceeds a simple midpoint, justifying a score of 7 for critical thinking that is clear and relevant, though lacking full analytical depth and critical rigor.

strengths: Clearly appropriate structure with logical headings and adherence to EE conventions.
In-text citations, pagination, and labelled figures are consistently applied.
Layout and formatting largely consistent with minor encoding issues.
weaknesses: Table of contents duplicates section numbers, causing navigation confusion.
Encoding glitches (HTML superscripts, stray symbols) appear in text and equations.
Figure captions lack detailed data sources and interpretation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work meets the criteria for band 3â4 with a generally appropriate structure and formatting, but minor lapses in numbering consistency and formatting glitches limit it to a score of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly stated verbatim on the title page and revisited in the methodology and conclusion.  It is narrow (one region, one programme, one set of external effects) and clearly anchored in positive-externality theory, demonstrating an effective connection to the forthcoming discussion.  Clarity could be marginally improved by simplifying wording ("recent investments for the improvement of â¦"), hence a solid 5 rather than full marks.

The student frames a highly specific Economics topic â the impact of first-1000-day investments on positive externalities in Val del Ceno â and maintains this focus throughout all sections (introduction, theory, primary research, economic analysis).  The purpose (measuring externalities and welfare loss) is consistently linked to the chosen community case study, making the investigation clearly suitable for systematic economic analysis.  Minor digressions (extended neuro-development background) prevent a flawless 6, but overall communication and appropriateness fit securely in the 5â6 band.

Key secondary literature (Heckman ROI estimates, WHO framework) is integrated into the argument and linked to diagrams illustrating MSB/MPB and LRAS/PPC shifts.  Primary survey findings are embedded in the welfare-loss discussion.  Some sources (Wikipedia entries, YouTube video) are less academic and occasionally cited for factual claims, lowering rigour.  Application is therefore âmostly relevantâ rather than consistently so, meriting the top of the 3â4 band.

The essay follows EE conventions (title page, abstract section absent but not compulsory, contents, sequential chapters, bibliography).  Headings and sub-headings create logical flow.  Minor issues (some duplicated section numbers; long blocks of text) prevent the highest band score, so a 3 is appropriate.

Word count, table of contents and in-text citations are present; diagrams are labelled; pagination is implied through the table.  Formatting is largely consistent, though there are a few encoding glitches (HTML superscripts, stray symbols).  Layout therefore meets core EE requirements with minor lapses, justifying a score of 3 within the top band.

A structured argument progresses from theory to context, survey findings, and welfare analysis, and the student explicitly recognises sample-size bias as a limitation.  Evaluation remains partialâalternative explanations, cost considerations and robustness checks are missingâso the work fits the âreasoned argument with partially critical evaluationâ description (band 4â6), receiving 4.

The analysis links theory to data through before/after diagrams and interprets survey percentages in welfare-loss terms, showing clear but not deep analytical treatment.  Causal inferences (foundation project â shift to Q_so) are asserted rather than rigorously tested, and numerical quantification of external benefits is absent.  Conclusions are âmostlyâ but not fully supported, aligning with the middle of the 4â6 range.

The student demonstrates clear understanding of market failure, positive externalities, welfare loss and ROI concepts, using correct diagrams and definitions.  Knowledge is coherent yet occasionally descriptive (extended narrative on brain development not tightly linked to economic content).  Use of sources to substantiate demographic context is only partially integrated into the economic reasoning, matching the 3â4 descriptor ceiling.

The majority of evidence (survey data, demographic statistics, Heckman studies) is appropriate to the RQ and clearly relevant to the analysis of externalities and welfare loss.  Gaps remainâno cost data for the foundationâs spending beyond a single figureâyet overall research quality fits securely in the 4â6 band, toward its upper end.

The essay employs secondary sources (Heckman papers, WHO, national statistics) and one primary instrument (survey of 57 parents).  The choice of a survey is justified and the sampling process described, showing informed selection.  However, the range is moderate (single survey, no cost-benefit data, no official childcare statistics) and limitations of the sample size are acknowledged but not addressed methodologically.  This aligns with the middle of the 3â4 band.

Economic terminology (MSB, MPC, LRAS, PPC, welfare loss, ROI) is used accurately and consistently in text and diagrams.  Occasional typographical slips ("$$\omega_{max}") do not impede meaning.  Breadth and accuracy correspond with the 5â6 band; a score of 5 reflects a few minor notation inconsistencies.

annotations.comment: Contextual data on Val del Ceno draws on Wikipediaâintegrate ISTAT statistics or official reports for demographic accuracy.
The first phase research is described clearly, showing systematic community engagement to identify local needs.
The research question is explicitly stated and clearly focused, effectively anchoring the investigation to positive-externality theory and the case study in Val del Ceno.
The word count is provided at the start of the document, meeting the EE formatting requirement to document length.
The investigationâs title is stated prominently at the start, providing clear identification of the subject and scope.
Consider simplifying the wording of the research question to improve readabilityâremove unnecessary qualifiers to make it more concise.
The table of contents repeats section 3 headings, creating confusion in navigationârevise numbering or headings for consistency.
The introduction provides extensive neurodevelopment detail, but the connection to economic analysis is tenuousâfocus more tightly on marketâfailure context.
Reliance on Wikipedia entries for factual claims undermines academic rigourâreplace with peerâreviewed or official sources.
The methodology section clearly outlines both secondary and primary data sources, demonstrating informed selection of relevant studies.
The survey sample of 57 parents is relatively small and may not represent the entire communityâconsider expanding the sample or triangulating with other data.
The chosen secondary sourceâHeckmanâs working paperâis highly authoritative and appropriate for anchoring the theoretical framework.
No quantitative costâbenefit analysis is presented for the foundationâs investmentâintroduce estimated figures to deepen analysis.
The definition of market failure is accurate and clearly stated, showing strong understanding of core economic concepts.
Citing a coursebook for definitions is acceptable, but supplement with journal articles to enhance scholarly depth.
Include explicit labels and units on the MPB/MSB diagram axes to improve clarity and avoid reader confusion.
The description of how individual consumption creates societal benefits demonstrates clear understanding of positive externality mechanics.
Selfâcreated diagrams need empirical calibrationâprovide data points or references to support the magnitude of curve shifts.
The ROI expression â7.3^{17}â appears to be a formatting errorâconfirm the exponent and correct to the intended benefitâcost ratio.
Relying on Wikipedia for the economistâs biography weakens source credibilityâuse the centerâs website or published CV instead.
Provide detailed figure captions, data sources, and interpretation for Figures 2 and 3 to enhance reader understanding.
Claims about reduced public expenditure lack numerical backingâintroduce estimated costâsaving figures for greater analytical depth.
The Heckman Curve explanation is concise and accurately conveys why early investment yields higher social returns.
Citing a YouTube video for the Heckman Curve is inappropriateâreference the original publication or an academic summary.
Notation $$\omega_{\rm{max}}$$ in the populationâloss section is misformattedâensure correct LaTeX rendering to avoid reader confusion.
Figure 5 caption superscript â1850^{30}â is unclearâsimplify or remove the exponent notation to enhance readability.
Quantify service shortfalls in the burden of childcare section to strengthen the economic case for intervention.
Project objectives are comprehensive, but lack measurable targetsâdefine specific metrics or timelines to evaluate success.
Survey design is well described, but perspectives from nonâparticipant community members are missingâconsider broader stakeholder inclusion.
Report summary statistics (e.g., means, SD) for demographic questions to improve transparency and analytical rigor.
Survey results chart shows strong engagement, but percentages should be linked explicitly to sample size for clarity.
The overall survey findings are highly positive, yet no discussion of selection bias or response bias is provided.
Figure 6 is well labeled with supply shifts and price change, effectively illustrating the market impact post-intervention.
The welfareâloss discussion links survey percentages to triangle area but lacks monetary valuationâestimate â¬ values to enrich the evaluation.
